evidence: goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go only defines transport action metadata and parsing utilities and performs no init-time mutation.
goseg/startram/errors.go exposes one stable connection error message and wraps an injected error through errpolicy without creating package-level state.
No shared mutable state or import-time side effects are evident in the assigned files.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Architectural coupling is low in scope, but action/action-meta registration is manual; adding a new namespace/action today can silently drift from protocol usage unless additional compile-time or golden tests enforce registry parity across packages.

evidence: The wireguard loading/config path is composed of multiple near-pass-through layers: `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` only forwards each public entrypoint to `container.*WithRuntime(...)`, and `goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go` builds a runtime struct by mechanically copying fields without domain-specific adaptation.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` defines adapter interfaces (`runtimeContainer`, `runtimeUrbit`, `runtimeConfig`, etc.) and default adapters whose methods are mostly one-to-one wrappers over concrete package functions.
Runtime configuration for WireGuard is spread across `goseg/defaults/defaults.go` and `goseg/config/wireguardbuilder/builder.go`, increasing duplicate ownership of default behavior and constants.

goseg/internal/seams/runtime.go contains duplicated seam helpers for many arities/signatures (e.g. `noopFallback1x2`, `noopFallback2x2`, `noopFallback2x3`, `DefaultIfNil1x2`, `DefaultIfNil2x3`, `RequireSeam1x2`, etc.), creating a parallel API surface that scales by copy-paste.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go and goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go add additional adapter wrappers (`adaptCopyFileToVolumeFn`, per-service runtime builders) to satisfy the seam indirection, increasing seam churn with each new service constructor.

goseg/config/config_update.go repeatedly re-implements identical field-by-field checks and mutations across `hasUpdates()` and `applyConfPatch()`, so adding one config field requires touching multiple large switch-like/if chains in lockstep.

findings.summary: Wireguard runtime abstractions form an extra indirection chain with little behavioral value, increasing call depth without adding policy.
evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` has a series of entrypoints (`loadWireguard`, `wgContainerConf`, `buildWgConf`, `writeWgConf`, `WriteWgConfToFile`, `copyWGFileToVolume`) that only delegate to container-layer functions with the same arguments after `wireguardRuntimeFromDocker` conversion.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go` constructs `container.WireguardRuntime` by direct field assignment from `dockerRuntime` with no transformation or invariant enforcement.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` default adapters (`runtimeContainerAdapter`, `runtimeUrbitAdapter`, `runtimeConfigAdapter`, etc.) mainly forward to concrete funcs (`StartContainer`, `GetLatestContainerInfo`, etc.) rather than adding abstraction behavior.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the wireguard orchestration seam into one seam boundary: either keep orchestration->runtime conversion in a single package boundary and expose only operation-level methods with real policy, or remove redundant forwarding layers where they only wrap/call-through. This reduces chain depth and makes dependency direction clearer for debugging and testing.

findings.summary: Wireguard defaults are defined in multiple independent modules, creating duplicate source-of-truth for default runtime/container behavior.
evidence: `goseg/defaults/defaults.go` defines a full `WgConfig` struct with concrete defaults (`WireguardVersion`, `Repo`, hashes, `CapAdd`, `Sysctls`, `Volumes`).

`goseg/config/wireguardbuilder/builder.go` reconstructs a `structs.WgConfig` manually and re-embeds similar default fields (`WireguardName`, `CapAdd`, `Volumes`, `Sysctls`) with only a few version-driven fields changed.
Because these are manually repeated, updates to one default definition do not automatically flow to the builder, making behavior divergence possible across codepaths.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical Wireguard default source and have `BuildConfig` derive all static defaults from it (then override only the version-dependent fields). Add a narrow builder that cannot drift from `defaults.WgConfig` unless explicitly changed.

findings.summary: Some runtime interfaces are introduced for test seams but expose almost no behavior boundary, limiting interface honesty.
evidence: Multiple runtime structs (`importerRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `supportRuntime`) use function fields for DI and call sites frequently use direct concrete functions through defaults, indicating interface-style seams are primarily mechanical.

`runtime_seams.go` and `wireguardRuntimeFromDocker`/`newDockerRuntime` assemble runtime bundles as pass-through function sets; many callers invoke wrappers that only enforce nil-checks before delegation.
In practical terms, this raises cognitive cost for tracing execution because concrete behavior is often reachable only after several adapter layers.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate seam strategy: keep coarse seams for major external boundaries, but avoid creating module-level interfaces/functions that only provide delegation. For each package, expose a single explicit runtime interface with cohesive methods where fallback behavior/translation is enforced.

findings.summary: Over-dense seam-helper boilerplate lowers abstraction leverage and increases maintenance cost.
evidence: `runtime.go` introduces many narrowly specialized helpers per arity (`noopFallback1`, `noopFallback2`, `DefaultIfNil1x2`, `RequireSeam2x2`, etc.) plus multiple typed variants that largely duplicate behavior.
`container_bridge.go` then adds adapter glue functions (`adaptCopyFileToVolumeFn`) to bridge slight signature differences rather than a single coherent seam contract.
The same pattern appears in tests and mocks, which amplifies the maintenance burden with each new seam shape.
findings.suggestion: Replace the arity-specific helper explosion with a small, explicit seam container object and shared no-op/default constructors (or a generator-like helper package) so adding a new seam only requires one adapter registration, not multiple boilerplate wrappers.

findings.summary: Config patch handling is split across duplicated manual field maps, making schema drift easy and refactors hard.
evidence: `ConfPatch` in `config_update.go` is a large flat field list where new configuration knobs require manually adding pointers plus duplicate handling.
`hasUpdates()` independently enumerates every patch field in a long disjunction while `applyConfPatch()` repeats a similar exhaustive field-by-field assignment pass.
This mirrors behavior in `version.go` where retry and request/state handling is centralized but patch-like validation logic is not shared across config paths.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a declarative patch registry (or explicit patch command types with per-field handlers) so new config fields are represented once and validation/update logic is co-located, preventing divergence between `hasUpdates` and `applyConfPatch`.

findings.summary: Module init paths spawn perpetual background work without explicit lifecycle handles.
evidence: `auth.Initialize` starts a goroutine that runs indefinitely (`CleanupStaleSessions`) without a stop/cancel path.
`system.InitializeWiFi` launches `wifiInfoLoop(device)` via `go` and never exposes a stop mechanism tied to shutdown context.
`logger.Initialize` performs global side-effectful initialization (`loggerInitOnce`, global `zap` replacement, stdout/ASCII output), making it hard to control lifecycle and test in embedded contexts.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit lifecycle APIs (`Start`/`Stop`, injected context, and testable runner interfaces) for auth and wifi polling so background tasks can be deterministically started/stopped and logger init is separated from runtime configuration.
